this is from Spaces
@all I stopped by the fields this morning to check in things, and everything was out and a mess. Can you all please relay to your coaches that when they are the last ones on the field be it practices or games a that we need to put our league items away and lock the mound cage? That includes rakes, mounds, chalkers, chalk, string, etc.

We can't control when other parties come in and leave trash, but we can control when all of our items aren't put back and locked.

Putting a Space together today for All coaches regarding field and gameday conditions, etc. WE can lead with this

Tyler Christofferson
Mon, Mar 16, 7:58 PM (7 days ago)
to Cole, Jason, Gayle, me

All, 

Below is the email I prepared to send to coaches, but I don't know how to bulk email, so I'm pleading for help. I have included some general field maintenance stuff at the end of this email that we have shared with coaches in the past.


Hi Coaches,

As we navigate this incredibly rainy start to the season, I want to touch base regarding the current state of our primary facilities. We’ve had very few breaks in the weather, and unfortunately, Lower Woodland fields 3, 4, 5, and 6 are struggling to drain.
We are seeing significant wear and tear in specific areas where the ground simply isn’t firm enough to handle the current level of traffic. Specifically, the turf near the batting cages is being pushed to the limit. I have included pictures of a few of the fields from today and a typical worn spot in the outfield grass.

Building Goodwill with Seattle Parks
The Parks Department has been a great partner, often deferring to our league officials once game play begins. To maintain that trust and ensure we have their support for the remainder of the season, we must show them we are responsible stewards of these fields during our practice windows.
Please implement the following adjustments immediately:
 * Batting Practice: If weather permits practicing, please find areas away from the cages for swings. If space allows, have players hit from the walking paths to save the turf until things dry out.
 * Outfield Drills: The grass along the foul lines is suffering. Please move warmups and drills to the deep outfield along the fences or into center field where the grass is holding up better.
 * Respecting Closures: If Parks has closed the fields, the dirt is likely unusable. Using "closed" dirt and leaving visible ruts or marks is a surefire way for the league to receive a formal complaint.
 * Player Safety: This is our top priority. Please choose locations for ground balls that are away from puddles and on solid, safe ground.

The "Friday Push" for Saturday Games
While cancellations of games and practices are frustrating, they are sometimes necessary for the long-term health of the fields and the safety of the kids. We appreciate your flexibility and ask that you keep your parents updated as conditions change. Looking ahead, we expect a break in the rain this Friday afternoon. We need a volunteer crew of coaches and team parents on-site Friday to help remove standing water. Getting that water off the dirt early will maximize our drying time and dramatically increase the chances of us playing our scheduled games on Saturday. Please keep a close eye on your email and Google Chats for real-time updates regarding Friday’s work party and weekend field status. 


Thank you for everything you do for the players and the league. Let's work together to keep these fields playable for the long haul.

![Field 3 condition](images/field-3-condition.png)
![Field 4 — standing water near home plate](images/field-4-standing-water.png)
![Field 5 — standing water near home plate](images/field-5-standing-water.png)
![Field 5 — alternate view](images/field-5-condition-alt.png)
![Field 6 condition](images/field-6-condition.png)
![Outfield grass wear](images/field-grass-wear.png)

Below are some notes that I have shared with coaches in previous years about maintaining our fields.

Closed Fields:
First thing is that if the parks department has closed the field due to weather, don't practice on the dirt. Oftentimes if it's not actively raining the grass is still a good place to get a workout for your team. The other note about a close field is that while you might not be able to practice on it, you can improve it and make it more likely that your team or other teams in the league can play on Saturday. When I was coaching AA, we had late Friday practice and then the 9am Saturday morning games almost every week, which meant there wasn't a lot of time to save a field from the overnight rain. Depending on the weather intensity, I would determine if I needed to rake the field Friday night, or if a touch up before my game Saturday morning would be enough.

Working the Field:
Generally this refers to raking the whole field, the problem areas, or where all the fielders stand. There should be rakes in the lock boxes at the fields, but it might be good for you to bring your own garden rake from home.
If the field is relatively dry on Friday night, the best thing you can do is scratch the field up with a rake (large field rake or garden rake). Think nail drag, turning the dirt over and bringing air to the soil in the hopes that overnight rain will be better absorbed. Ideally this is something the parks department would do everyday, but I don't see that happening. Smooth out the holes, try and fill them. Bring dirt from the high spots to the low spots. When most people are trying to fix a field, it's wet and they push all the puddles to the dry spots or into the grass, and that's just fine. Problem is that it takes dirt from the already low areas and pushes it to the higher areas. And no one ever brings that dirt back so we make our low high traffic areas worse. When the field is dry or just damp, that's a great time to try and level it out.
If the field is wet, but it's not supposed to rain much from Friday night into Saturday:
Remove all puddles. (Shop-vac, buckets, spreading with a rake or broom)
Use a rake to scratch up the surface cutting into the dirt as much as a 1/2inch. Turn the dirt over and leave the scratch marks. The wind will help dry out the field overnight.
DO NOT put turface or diamond dry on the field the night before a game! It wastes the limited supply we have, and is better used right before a game after the field has naturally dried out overnight. More on that below.
Check the hourly weather forecasts and radar. Don't do a bunch of work if it's just going to dump rain all night. The fields cant take much water, we need periods of dry to scratch it up.
Saturday morning. Depending on how much you want to play, or care that the AAA and Majors baseball and softball teams play, get to the field early. I am regularly there by 7am if the field is in bad condition and I recruit parents or coaches to help me. Same principles apply, If it's wet, keep scratching the field with rakes and remove puddles. It's amazing how much scratching the field and some wind will dry it out just enough to get a game in. 45min from your game, start putting turface or diamond dry on the wet spots, fielding spots, home plate, in front of the mound etc. We have four fields and a limited supply of turface. If you see a Seattle Parks person there, flag them down and let them know that we have games all day and if they could bring more turface, it would help! They love to help coaches that are there working the field. Also be reasonable on the possibility to save your field. Don't waste product. Field 3 drains the best and is usually easy to save. Field 4 has problem areas at first base, and home plate, but can often be saved. Fields 5 and 6 need extra love and prayers.
Turface/Diamond Dry
Often confused as the same product when you listen to MLB broadcasts, but they are different and they do different things. Turface company makes both products, but diamond dry is an older product that is chalky or powder like and is strictly used in wet field management. The gronular turface looks like kitty litter (its baked clay) and is the main surfacing product on professional fields. Turface also soaks up a lot of water. If I had the choice between the two, I would use turface over diamond dry because it is the product that should be on a field even when it's dry. So go to the Turface first and fall back on diamond dry if you run out.
DO NOT fill a puddle hole with either product. Remove the water first, scratch it up, and then apply the drying agent. Dumping a turface on a puddle will not save the day for you. Put in the time to remove the puddle and you will be much better off.
Spreading the turface is relatively easy. I like to find the end of the bag that has an area where I can pull the top open. I open it up to create maybe a 6in by 6in hole, lift the back and start pouring the turface out while I'm walking and swinging the bag side to side spreading the product. This will help you even out the playing surface, and save you from having to rake turface from one spot to the other. Once you get it on the ground, smooth it out with a rake. You can still use the teeth of the rake to scratch the surface as you spread it around.
Email Me:
Email me Friday when you get to your practice a picture of the field if we have rain or are expecting it. I'll probably be planning to go work in my field anyway. If you let me know the condition of the fields, I can notify AAA and Majors and we can work as a group to fix up the fields.

Hopefully that's a good place to start. If you have questions, ask away. If you have a dry field and parks isn't there, put down the foul lines yourself. Use a few nails and string in the lockboxes. One nail in the outfield grass on the foul side of the paint line, and one nail just past home plate, set so the string line goes through through the point at the back of the plate. You place the chalk down on the fair side (field side) so that it goes right under 1st and 3rd base. The string line should also be right along the edge of 1st and 3rd bases. Parks isn't always good about striping the outfield, so sometimes our foul lines are more angular.

--
Tyler Christofferson
NCLL Fields
